prompt: Aerial close-up photograph shot directly overhead of an ancient textile laid flat, transitioning from dark rich earth on the left to pale dry cracked stone on the right. The left half is extraordinarily dense — hundreds of tightly woven threads each a visibly distinct color, no two adjacent threads sharing the same hue, ochre, indigo, rust, sage, copper, violet, crimson, ash — each thread carrying its own tiny geometric micro-pattern, small irregular motifs suggesting distinct human-made textile traditions from different cultures, packed so richly the surface is almost illegible. Moving from left to right, threads progressively disappear — entire colors vanishing completely, their micro-patterns lost with them, subtle vertical fault lines where groups of threads have already gone missing, then bare warp with no weft, then scattered single threads. The far right edge is almost entirely bare pale cracked stone with only a few ghost threads remaining and isolated severed thread ends scattered on the surface, disconnected from the weave entirely. The transition from overwhelming human richness to near-emptiness is the entire subject. No framing, no decoration, no text, no labels. Forensic overhead documentation aesthetic. Medium format film texture. Negative prompt: symmetric, centered, uniform, decorative, text, labels, diagrams, borders, people, charts, monochrome, nature, leaves, roots, plants, ecology
dataset_name: UNESCO Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger
insight: Approximately 2,500 languages are currently endangered, and each extinction permanently destroys a unique cognitive and cultural architecture with no possibility of recovery.
